data.title: Market Chatter: Ping An Healthcare and Technology Utilizes DeepSeek's Model in Diagnosis Platform
data.description:
Ping An Healthcare and Technology has embedded DeepSeek's artificial intelligence model into its own AI model and a platform for doctors, The Standard reported Friday.

Access to DeepSeek's model will help with more accurate diagnoses of diseases and personalized health management, the report said.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Relicab Cable Manufacturing Bags INR230 Million Export Order from Gulf Client
data.description:
Relicab Cable Manufacturing received an export order worth about 230 million Indian rupees from an unnamed Gulf-region client, according to an Indian bourse filing on Friday.

The order includes solid, flat, flexible, and round cables, with execution set for 30 days.

data.title: Rheinmetall's Midterm Guidance Is Conservative — Market Talk
data.description: Rheinmetall's midterm guidance and estimates are conservative as they only reflect the order pipeline based on current defense spending pledges, despite clear signals of an impending spending surge, HSBC analysts Dario Dickmann and Christoph Dolleschal say. This means Rheinmetall should experience continued upgrades of midterm estimates and targets in the years to come, they add. The analysts don't think a ceasefire in the Ukraine-Russia war would materially change the arms maker's midterm picture, because there are no indications that Ukraine-related sales or EU defense spending would decrease. HSBC raised its share-price target for Rheinmetall to 1,000 euros from 700 euros. Shares are up 5.15% at 796 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Newell Brands Maintains Dividend at $0.07 per Share, Payable March 14 to Holders as of Feb. 28
Modi-Trump Meeting Sends Some Positive Signs While Trade Risks Persist — Market Talk
data.description: The outcome of the Modi-Trump meeting aligns with expectations, with near-term trade frictions set to persist, but a strategic partnership possible over the medium term, according to Nomura analysts. Discussions centered around a trade deal, India increasing energy imports from the U.S., defense cooperation and a reiteration of the strategic partnership, Sonal Varma and Aurodeep Nandi write in a report. Negotiations on a U.S.-India trade deal will begin, with some concessions likely, they say. Talks are expected to focus on India purchasing more U.S. oil & gas, renewable energy and defense equipment, they add. "But given India's higher tariff rate, high non-tariff barriers and its trade surplus with the U.S., it remains in Trump's line of fire on reciprocal tariffs," they write. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0455 ET - Friday's fall in the dollar is unlikely to be sustained as tariff uncertainty remains, MUFG Bank analyst Lee Hardman says in a note. The U.S. currency hits a two-month low against a basket of currencies on relief that President Trump's plans for reciprocal tariffs won't be implemented until at least April. However, reciprocal tariffs will be based not only on tariffs imposed by other countries on U.S. imports but also other factors such as VAT, exchange rates and regulations. "We are more wary of a more disruptive outcome now that non-tariff factors will be included," Hardman says. Trade-policy uncertainty should remain supportive for the dollar, he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.4% to a two-month low of 106.831. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0418 ET - Oil prices edge higher in early trade after U.S. President Trump delayed immediate plans to impose reciprocal tariffs, easing fears of a trade war that could hurt global growth and demand for crude. Brent is up 0.6% at $75.48 a barrel, while WTI rises 0.5% to $71.64 a barrel. Trump's decision to have federal agencies study how to adjust tariffs to match those of other countries by April 1 provided some temporary relief to the market and pushed down the U.S. dollar. Meanwhile, the IEA lifted its oil-demand growth forecast and lowered its expectations for a global oil surplus this year, providing further support to market sentiment. Still, gains continue to be capped by persistent fears of escalating trade tensions and prospects of a potential peace deal between Russia and Ukraine. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0337 ET - Heightened uncertainty over the trade tariffs U.S. President Trump could implement means investors need to ensure they have diverse portfolios and employ hedging strategies, says UBS Global Wealth Management's Mark Haefele. "While the delay in implementation limits immediate market impact, the risk of retaliation remains," the chief investment officer says. Within equities, capital preservation strategies could be necessary to prevent losses if stocks turn lower. UBS GWM favors high-grade and investment-grade bonds as a way to diversify portfolios as these offer "insulation against uncertainty." (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0332 ET - The euro could rise further on relief that President Trump's latest tariff plans aren't immediate, ING analyst Chris Turner says in a note. With tariffs to be assessed on a country-by-country basis in a report expected on April 1, trading partners have time to fine-tune strategies. However, the outcome could be "eye-wateringly large tariffs against some of the key countries with which the U.S. runs a goods deficit." The EU will "certainly be in the cross-hairs." That means the euro's gains should be limited to $1.0535-$1.0575 near-term, and it still risks falling to parity in the second quarter, he says. The euro rises to a two-and-a-half-week high of $1.0482, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0253 ET - The dollar falls to a two-month low against a basket of currencies after President Trump announced plans for reciprocal tariffs to be assessed on a country-by-country basis as opposed to blanket tariffs. Such a process should "inevitably take some time" with investigations expected to complete by April 1 and remedies implemented immediately after, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. "In the meantime, questions whether this tariff threat will be used as a negotiating tool are likely to linger." The limited tariff news and lower yields after Thursday's U.S. producer prices data dented the dollar, they say. The DXY dollar index falls to a low of 106.962. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0250 ET - U.S. Treasurys trade little changed in early European hours after President Trump ordered federal agencies on Thursday to explore how to adjust U.S. tariffs to match of those of other countries. Treasurys rallied Thursday, fully reversing Wednesday's CPI-triggered selloff. That said, Trump's tariffs measures continue to hang over markets. "The threat of those reciprocal tariffs, plus apparent upcoming announcements on further tariffs on cars, chips, and pharmaceutical products will thus hang over proceedings like the 'sword of Damocles' for a while yet," says Pepperstone's Michael Brown in a note. This creates choppy trading conditions and higher volatility, the senior research strategist says. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield last trades 2 basis points higher at 4.545%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0249 ET - The U.K.'s FTSE 100 index is expected to open slightly higher, up 0.1%, according to IG, while Germany's DAX looks set to open 0.4% lower after hitting record highs recently. The recent rally in stocks has been helped by earnings data, says XTB's Kathleen Brooks in a note. Traders have largely shrugged off tariff threats for now, but the prospect of Trump announcing tariffs against European goods still looms. President Trump ordered federal agencies on Thursday to explore how to adjust U.S. tariffs to match of those of other countries. Brooks says these could take longer to plan. France's CAC 40 is expected to open down 0.1%, according to IG. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

1752 ET - AUD/USD has strengthened this week, and now trades above 0.6300, having fallen below 0.6250 at one point, but it remains vulnerable because "peak tariff" has not been reached yet, says Joe Capurso, economist at CBA. U.S. President Trump has signaled the implementation of more tariffs on imports and even if Australia can convince the U.S. government to not impose tariffs, some Australian exports to third economies are part of the export supply chain to the U.S., he adds. CBA estimates 2.4% of Australian GDP satisfies U.S. domestic demand through direct and indirect exports, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1523 ET [Dow Jones]--The perceived frontrunner to become Canada's Liberal Party leader and next prime minister, Mark Carney, says there could be a national election as early as mid-March, after party members pick a new leader. Political analysts believe there likely will be an election in the spring, as the opposition parties have vowed to take down the minority Liberal government when parliament resumes March 24. Recent polling shows momentum for the Liberals, with the Conservative Party lead narrowing. Carney says any decision will reflect where things stand with the Trump administration's tariff threats. "If parliament needs to be recalled for certain reasons, it will be. If it makes sense to get a strong mandate at that point, that is what will follow," says Carney, the former Canadian and UK central banker. (paul.vieira@wsj.com)

1433 ET - Fixed-income portfolio managers should avoid taking outsized bets and stick to the duration their clients need, as markets face increased uncertainty, says PGIM's Gregory Peters. "We are in a higher inflation regime and consequently we are in a higher rate regime," he says. Peters says Trump tariffs may increase inflation in the short term, but longer term he is afraid they could hamper growth. Meanwhile, taking risks doesn't seem to be paying off, he says. Peters likes Treasurys over corporates as spreads are tight. "The concern is on the valuation side, that I'm not getting paid for any risks." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1334 ET - UBS expects Wayfair's value proposition to shine given recent investments and continued efforts to right-size cost structure. The analysts say in a research note that the moves could drive "a powerful P&L inflection ahead." As for the impact of higher tariffs on Chinese goods, the analysts say the company is in a better position than other traditional home furnishings players. UBS estimates 50% to 60% Wayfair's sales are sourced from China. Since it has a marketplace model, the analysts say Wayfair should be able to pass along any incremental costs from tariffs to the consumer through higher retail prices. They also point out that Wayfair's large vendor base of over 20,000 suppliers would allow it to shift its source of supply to other companies outside of China. Wayfair rises 3.1%. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1252 ET - Crocs has looked at tariffs and run the numbers. The clog and sandal company's guidance for 1Q and 2025 embeds an additional 10% tariff on goods imported from China as well as the anticipated additional 25% tariff on goods imported from Mexico beginning in March, says CFO Susan Healy on an earnings call. She notes that the company doesn't have any production in Canada. "In 2025, we expect the share of enterprise imports into the US from China to be approximately 15%, with Crocs at 10% and Heydude at 27%," says Healy, referring to specific brands. "Our exposure to Mexico is expected to be under 4% and for the Crocs brand only." Crocs surges 19% as 4Q results beat estimates. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0955 GMT - Friday's fall in the dollar is unlikely to be sustained as tariff uncertainty remains, MUFG Bank analyst Lee Hardman says in a note. The U.S. currency hits a two-month low against a basket of currencies on relief that President Trump's plans for reciprocal tariffs won't be implemented until at least April. However, reciprocal tariffs will be based not only on tariffs imposed by other countries on U.S. imports but also other factors such as VAT, exchange rates and regulations. "We are more wary of a more disruptive outcome now that non-tariff factors will be included," Hardman says. Trade-policy uncertainty should remain supportive for the dollar, he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.4% to a two-month low of 106.831. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0954 GMT - The outcome of the Modi-Trump meeting aligns with expectations, with near-term trade frictions set to persist, but a strategic partnership possible over the medium term, according to Nomura analysts. Discussions centered around a trade deal, India increasing energy imports from the U.S., defense cooperation and a reiteration of the strategic partnership, Sonal Varma and Aurodeep Nandi write in a report. Negotiations on a U.S.-India trade deal will begin, with some concessions likely, they say. Talks are expected to focus on India purchasing more U.S. oil & gas, renewable energy and defense equipment, they add. "But given India's higher tariff rate, high non-tariff barriers and its trade surplus with the U.S., it remains in Trump's line of fire on reciprocal tariffs," they write. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0940 GMT - China's latest credit and lending data suggests policymakers are still struggling to provide much of a lift to broad credit growth, Capital Economics says. China economist Zichun Huang attributes the January headline figures showing new local-currency loans at record highs to the usual seasonal pattern, noting that net lending is always strongest at the start of the year. To cut through the seasonality, CE looks at the data in terms of on-year growth in credit outstanding. On that basis, bank lending slowed from December to a fresh low last month, Huang writes. In the coming quarters, she thinks robust government bond issuance can lend support, but weak private demand will likely keep credit growth subdued. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0938 GMT - The Russian central bank is expected to hold interest rates at 21% in a decision at 1030 GMT as the ruble's recent recovery has likely helped tackle inflation, Commerzbank analyst Tatha Ghose says in a note. The currency's weakness after the last round of U.S. sanctions on Russia meant the central bank had to use large rate rises to counter the inflation pass-through, he says. The ruble has recovered substantially since then for "reasons not entirely clear," given USD/RUB and EUR/RUB are technical fixes that don't trade like normal floating exchange rates. However, cautious optimism over a Ukraine ceasefire deal could have offered some support. USD/RUB rises 0.4% to 89.8250 after hitting a five-month low of 89.2564 Thursday, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0918 GMT - Oil prices edge higher in early trade after U.S. President Trump delayed immediate plans to impose reciprocal tariffs, easing fears of a trade war that could hurt global growth and demand for crude. Brent is up 0.6% at $75.48 a barrel, while WTI rises 0.5% to $71.64 a barrel. Trump's decision to have federal agencies study how to adjust tariffs to match those of other countries by April 1 provided some temporary relief to the market and pushed down the U.S. dollar. Meanwhile, the IEA lifted its oil-demand growth forecast and lowered its expectations for a global oil surplus this year, providing further support to market sentiment. Still, gains continue to be capped by persistent fears of escalating trade tensions and prospects of a potential peace deal between Russia and Ukraine. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0915 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds climb after Thursday's above-forecast U.K. GDP data caused markets to lower their expectations of interest-rate cuts from the Bank of England. Markets now only price in an 18% probability of a BOE rate cut in March, down from a 27% chance priced in earlier this week, LSEG Refinitiv data show. "Front-end pricing for [monetary] easing is increasingly centered on May and August," Barclay's Moyeen Islam says in a note. The 10-year gilt yield rises 3 basis points to 4.511%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0900 GMT - Beijing may not be in a hurry to intensify property support after signs of resilience in the sector in recent months, DBS analysts say in a research note. China's property sales performance in 30 key cities remained decent in January while home prices also showed some improvement, they say. That suggests that the upcoming policy window for more stimulus at the National People's Congress in March could be delayed to late April, they say. However, local governments continue to face obstacles in buying back excess housing inventory to convert into affordable rental housing as they would need to purchase unsold units at a more than 30% discount to the average selling price in order to break even, DBS estimates. Developers might not be willing to take such a sharp discount and the rate of success in buying up unsold housing remains low so far, they say. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0856 GMT - The Romanian central bank is likely to leave interest rates unchanged at 6.5% in a decision later but this will probably have little impact on the leu, ING's Frantisek Taborsky. Unchanged rates are widely expected and the leu's moves are contained by the central bank under its managed-floating exchange rate regime, he says. The central bank has no reason to make any changes to its foreign exchange rate strategy in the first half. "If the situation calms down globally and locally on the fiscal and political side, we believe there may be room to devalue the leu in the second half of the year." EUR/RON trades flat at 4.9767. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0832 GMT - The euro could rise further on relief that President Trump's latest tariff plans aren't immediate, ING analyst Chris Turner says in a note. With tariffs to be assessed on a country-by-country basis in a report expected on April 1, trading partners have time to fine-tune strategies. However, the outcome could be "eye-wateringly large tariffs against some of the key countries with which the U.S. runs a goods deficit." The EU will "certainly be in the cross-hairs." That means the euro's gains should be limited to $1.0535-$1.0575 near-term, and it still risks falling to parity in the second quarter, he says. The euro rises to a two-and-a-half-week high of $1.0482, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0800 GMT - Malaysia's 2025 GDP growth is likely to slow this year, though domestic demand will stay strong and exports will moderate but remain resilient, OCBC senior Asean economist Lavanya Venkateswaran says in a note. She sees the economy growing at 4.5% versus 5.1% last year. Household and investment spending should remain firm, while electrical exports could grow at a slower pace, she reckons. External risks have risen with potential U.S. tariffs targeting Malaysia's trade surplus, she adds. Inflation is expected to rise to 2.7% in 2025 from 1.8% in 2024 due to the fuel subsidy reforms, but underlying pressures should stay contained, she says. The central bank is likely to keep rates at 3.00% in 2025, though a hike remains possible if it sees strong signs of persistent demand-side inflation, she adds. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0755 GMT - Beijing is likely to keep China's growth target at around 5% this year, Citi economists write in a note. The National People's Congress meeting, which starts March 5, will approve economic targets and the fiscal budget for 2025. The NPC targets won't be conditional on U.S.-China trade relations, as all the policy decisions are likely already made, they add. China is expected to maintain ambitious employment targets, while the consumer inflation target could be trimmed to around 2% for the first time since 2003 to reflect the low inflation reality in China, they say. Citi still expects fiscal expenditure to expand by 2.5 trillion yuan. With the PBOC staying in wait-and-see mode, Citi forecasts 50 bps policy rate cuts and a 100bp reduction in the reserve requirement ratio this year, starting from 2Q. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0753 GMT - The dollar falls to a two-month low against a basket of currencies after President Trump announced plans for reciprocal tariffs to be assessed on a country-by-country basis as opposed to blanket tariffs. Such a process should "inevitably take some time" with investigations expected to complete by April 1 and remedies implemented immediately after, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. "In the meantime, questions whether this tariff threat will be used as a negotiating tool are likely to linger." The limited tariff news and lower yields after Thursday's U.S. producer prices data dented the dollar, they say. The DXY dollar index falls to a low of 106.962. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Dollar's Fall on Tariff News Unlikely to Last — Market Talk
data.description: Friday's fall in the dollar is unlikely to be sustained as tariff uncertainty remains, MUFG Bank analyst Lee Hardman says in a note. The U.S. currency hits a two-month low against a basket of currencies on relief that President Trump's plans for reciprocal tariffs won't be implemented until at least April. However, reciprocal tariffs will be based not only on tariffs imposed by other countries on U.S. imports but also other factors such as VAT, exchange rates and regulations. "We are more wary of a more disruptive outcome now that non-tariff factors will be included," Hardman says. Trade-policy uncertainty should remain supportive for the dollar, he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.4% to a two-month low of 106.831. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: FX strengthens amid relief over tariffs, hawkish data
data.description:
The Hungarian forint and Polish zloty firmed on Friday, amid relief that U.S. President Donald Trump stopped short of immediately implementing new tariffs and fresh signs that monetary policy in central Europe will likely remain tight.

Trump tasked his economics team on Thursday with devising plans for reciprocal tariffs on every country taxing U.S. imports.

However, the directive stopped short of imposing fresh tariffs, instead kicking off what could be weeks or months of investigation into the levies imposed on U.S. goods by other trading partners and then devising a response.

The Hungarian forint firmed 0.34% versus the euro to 401.20, its strongest level since late-October, while the Polish zloty was 0.22% firmer at 4.168, hovering near 7-year-highs.

"(CEE currencies) are pretty strong on the back of the delay on these tariffs, which pushed the dollar weaker, and so other currencies are stronger," said a Warsaw-based FX trader.

"Then we have a chance for some news on the weekend regarding the (Ukraine) peace plan."

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy is expected to meet U.S. Vice President JD Vance in Munich on Friday after Trump startled U.S. allies by calling Vladimir Putin and announcing the start of talks to end the war in Ukraine.

Central European currencies have been supported by signals from central bankers that they will be cautious about cutting the cost of credit amid persistently high inflation.

Hungary's central bank will have to raise its 2025 inflation forecast after higher-than-expected January figures, the bank's director, Andras Balatoni, said late on Thursday.

Meanwhile in Poland, inflation came in above estimates in January at 5.3% year-on-year, data showed on Friday, while in Romania it was in line at 4.95%.

In Romania, investors were looking ahead to a central bank meeting on Friday that analysts expect will see the cost of credit remain on hold at 6.5%, the EU's joint-highest level alongside neighbouring Hungary.

The Romanian leu and Czech crown were flat at 4.9769 and 25.0480 respectively.

data.title: South Africa sells 1 billion rand of inflation-linked bonds at auction
data.description:
South Africa's government sold 1 billion rand ($54.41 million) of its 2031 (ZAI2031=), 2046 (ZAI2046=) and 2058 (ZAI2058=) inflation-linked bonds at auction, central bank data showed on Friday.

For the auction results, click here and scroll to Inflation-Linked Government Bond Auctions.

($1 = 18.3796 rand)

data.title: China January bank lending hits record high on policy stimulus
data.description:
Key points:
  • Jan new loans 5.13 trln yuan vs f'cast 4.50 trln yuan
  • But year-on-year growth slowed
  • Jan M2 money supply +7.0% y/y, vs f'cast of +7.2%
  • C.bank pledges to adjust policy stance to support growth

New bank loans in China surged more than expected to an all-time high in January as the central bank moved to shore up a patchy economic recovery, reinforcing expectations for more stimulus in the coming months.

Chinese banks extended 5.13 trillion yuan ($706.40 billion) in new yuan loans in January, more than quadrupling the December figure, data from the People's Bank of China showed on Friday, beating analysts' forecasts

Analysts polled by Reuters had predicted new yuan loans would rise to 4.5 trillion yuan last month, up sharply from 990 billion yuan in December and compared with 4.92 trillion yuan a year earlier - the previous record.

Chinese banks usually rush to lend at the beginning of the year as they compete for higher-quality customers and win market share, but lingering economic uncertainty continues to weigh on credit demand.

New bank lending totalled 18.09 trillion yuan last year, down from a record 22.75 trillion yuan in 2023 and hitting the lowest level since 2019, as businesses and consumers remained cautious about taking on more debt amid an uncertain economic outlook.

The economy grew 5% in 2024, meeting the government's official target, but the post-pandemic recovery has been patchy, with exports and manufacturing making up for weak domestic consumption.

Beijing is expected to maintain a growth target of around 5% this year, but analysts are uncertain over how quickly policymakers can revive sluggish domestic demand, even as U.S. President Donald Trump's aggressive trade measures pile more uncertainty on Chinese exporters.

To sustain growth and counter rising external pressures, Beijing has pledged higher fiscal spending, increased debt issuance and further monetary easing.

The central bank said on Thursday it would adjust its monetary policy at the appropriate time and use policy tools such as interest rates and bank reserve requirement ratio to support the economy, amid rising external headwinds.

China is now facing a renewed trade war with the United States after President Donald Trump slapped sweeping 10% tariffs on all Chinese imports.

In response, Beijing announced tariffs up to 15% on some U.S. imports starting February 10.

Stil, the measures were more modest than markets had feared, raising hopes there was room for negotiating.

Since September, Beijing has stepped up efforts to get the economy back on track, including interest rate cuts, a 10 trillion yuan debt relief package for local government, and tax incentives to spur demand in the crisis-hit property market.

Broad M2 money supply grew 7.0% from a year earlier, the central bank data showed, below analysts' 7.2% forecast in a Reuters poll. In December, M2 expanded 7.3%.

Outstanding yuan loans rose 7.5% in January from a year earlier, down from the 7.6% pace in December. Analysts had expected 7.3% growth.

($1 = 7.2622 Chinese yuan renminbi)

data.title: China's January, February iron ore imports to slide as cyclones hit Australian supply
data.description:
Key points:
  • China's Jan-Feb iron ore imports could fall at least 10%-analysts
  • Shipments from Australia to drop by up to 20 mln tons - analysts
  • BHP, Rio Tinto, Fortescue pause port operations

Iron ore imports to top consumer China for the first two months of 2025 are set to slide as weeks of tropical cyclones and bad weather in major producer Australia hit shipments, according to analysts.

Imports of the key steelmaking ingredient in January and February are forecast to be at least 10% less than the 209.45 million metric tons notched over the same period last year, analysts said.

Ship tracking service Kpler sees Chinese iron ore imports for January and February at 191.7 million tons, down 10.4% year-on-year.

Cyclones and bad weather mean shipments from Australia, the source of almost two-thirds of China's iron ore, are forecast to drop by 8 million tons-20 million tons in the first two months of 2025.

"Since it's a cyclone at category five, mining area activities, port infrastructures and marine transport might all be affected, so the actual impact might be higher than the current estimate," said Pei Hao, senior analyst at international brokerage Freight Investor Services (FIS).

Official import data for January and February will be published on March 7. China combines the data for the two months into one release to smooth out the impact of the Lunar New Year holidays.

Rio Tinto said last month it expects its first-quarter shipments to be impacted by heavy rainfall following cyclone Sean and that it would update the market at its results.

That was before the latest tropical cyclone Zelia forced the miner to suspend port operations this week. Zelia made landfall on Australia's west coast on Friday.

BHP and Fortescue have paused their Port Hedland operations for safety. Fortescue said it had also closed its Iron Bridge mining operations and canceled non-essential travel to the Pilbara sites.

Rio Tinto said it had cleared its Cape Lambert and Dampier port operations and there were no longer any ships or trains operating at its ports.

Fears of supply disruptions have helped stem falling prices caused by U.S. President Donald Trump's new tariff threat.

But the upside room for ore prices is limited with a ceiling at around $115 a ton, as Trump's new tariffs are expected to cast some shadow on demand, FIS' Pei said.

data.title: Haseko's Nine-Month Profit Drops Nearly 54%
data.description:
Haseko's profit attributable to owners dropped nearly 54% to 18.4 billion yen in the nine months ended Dec. 31, 2024, from 39.6 billion a year ago.

The construction firm's earnings per share fell to 67.28 yen from 145.16 yen a year ago, according to a Tokyo Stock Exchange filing on Thursday.

Net sales increased 8.8% to 836.7 billion yen from 768.9 billion yen in the previous year.

In a separate filing, Haseko said it incurred extraordinary losses, including impairment losses of 14.88 billion yen, a 2.72 billion yen loss on investment securities, and a 3.06 billion yen provision for litigation losses in its overseas-related business.

As such, it revised its earnings forecast for the fiscal year ending March 31 to an attributable profit of 30 billion yen or 109.87 yen per share from 53 billion yen or 194.29 yen per share previously.

Haseko's shares lost 2% at market close.

data.title: Youon Technology Subsidiaries Obtain High-tech Enterprise Certifications
data.description:
Youon Technology said its subsidiaries received high-tech enterprise certifications from Jiangsu provincial authorities, granting them tax benefits for three years.

Kexin Yongan renewed its high-tech enterprise certification, while holding subsidiary Hydrogen Energy Technology earned its first such certification, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

The certifications make both entities eligible for a reduced corporate income tax rate of 15% from 2024 to 2026.

Shares of the technology company closed 3% higher Friday.

data.title: ING ups Lotus to 'buy' on unfazed belief in 'Belgian gem'
data.description:
** ING upgrades Lotus Bakeries to "buy" from "hold," saying it remains convinced "by this Belgian gem's unique self-funded organic growth story in the Snacking segment"

** Shares in Lotus have dropped around 15% year-to-date on worries over its production capacity as demand for its popular Biscoff biscuit outstrips supply

** ING, however, sees the company's valuation multiple de-rating as "unwarranted" and "an attractive entry point", and sticks to its PT of EUR 12,000

** It points to unchanged fundamentals, limited exposure to surging cocoa prices, and no material risks related to the new U.S. administration

** The broker is also upbeat on long-term production, noting the "24/7 production shifts to ensure demand is supplied" and the opening of a new factory in Thailand by mid-2026

** Out of seven analysts that cover Lotus Bakeries, ​one rates the stock "buy," "​​five rate it "hold" and one rates it "sell"

data.title: Ubisoft's Guidance Confirmation Is Encouraging — Market Talk
data.description: Ubisoft Entertainment's confirmation of financial targets is encouraging since its fiscal year closes at the end of next month, UBS analysts write in a research note. The French videogame maker is forecasting net bookings of roughly 1.9 billion euros, break-even non-IFRS operating profit and free cash flow. The analysts attribute confirmation of those targets to revenue that Ubisoft could generate from partnerships as well as from videogame "Assassin's Creed Shadows," for which pre-orders have been solid so far. Ubisoft said pre-orders for the upcoming release are in line with those of "Assassin's Creed Odyssey," the second-most successful entry of the franchise. Ubisoft shares trade 2.4% higher at 11.40 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: SWS Research Starts Kingsoft Cloud at Buy with HK$12.98 Price Target
UOB Kay Hian Downgrades The United Laboratories International Holdings to Hold from Buy; Price Target is HK$13
data.description: The United Laboratories International Holdings has an average rating of outperform and price targets ranging from HK$10.25 to HK$15, according to analysts polled by Capital IQ.

data.title: Norway's PoLight Secures New Order for Augmented Reality Technology
data.description:
PoLight received an order worth 729,000 Norwegian kroner from an original equipment manufacturer, according to a Thursday release.

The transaction includes the supply of the Norway-based tunable optics technology company's TWedge wobulator evaluation kits, which will be used for augmented reality applications.

Delivery of the kits will take place within the first quarter.

data.title: Tibet Weixinkang Medicine's Subsidiary Secures Drug Registration for Bronchitis Drug
data.description:
Tibet Weixinkang Medicine said its subsidiary, Yangpu Jingtai Pharmaceutical, received a drug registration certificate from China's NMPA for inhalable acetylcysteine solution, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai Bourse.

The drug, used for respiratory conditions like bronchitis and emphysema, has completed regulatory approval and is preparing for market launch.

data.title: Hermes Sets High Bar for 2025 — Market Talk
data.description: Hermes International ended 2024 on a high note, with sales growth accelerating in the final quarter of the year, which sets a high bar for 2025, Stifel's Rogerio Fujimori says in a note. The French luxury group posted an 18% increase in sales at constant currency in the fourth quarter, accelerating from growth of 11% in the third and significantly ahead of consensus expectations, the Stifel analyst says. "The only negatives we see for 2025 are the high comparison base, further adverse FX hedging impact in [the first half] and additional 350 million-euro taxes in France--hopefully temporary," the analyst says. Shares rise 2.6%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

data.title: Birkin Bag Maker Hermes Posts Higher Revenue on Americas Strength — Update
data.description:
By Andrea Figueras

Birkin bag maker Hermes reported higher sales for the final quarter of 2024 boosted by a strong performance in the Americas, adding to signs of a rebound in demand for luxury goods.

The French luxury group on Friday reported revenue of 3.96 billion euros ($4.15 billion) for the fourth quarter, up nearly 18% at constant exchange rates compared with the prior-year period.

The result compares with analysts' expectations of 3.73 billion euros, according to a Visible Alpha poll of estimates.

Shares rose as much as 5% in early trading in Europe, with sector bellwether LVMH and Gucci owner Kering also up more than 1.5%.

Hermes's update follows Cartier owner Richemont and U.K. luxury group Burberry flagging improving trends in the U.S., which emerged as a bright spot for luxury groups amid a consumer pullback in China.

Hermes reported revenue increases across all regions in the quarter, with Japan and the Americas growing the fastest at 22% when excluding currency movements.

The company joined the elite club of those smashing fourth-quarter forecasts, Bernstein analyst Luca Solca said in a note.

The earnings season fueled hopes among investors that a sector recovery could be under way, with strong demand in the U.S. lifting luxury groups out of the doldrums after a postpandemic spending boom came to an end. China, which drove the industry's growth for years, has been a weak spot lately.

In Asia excluding Japan, Hermes reported a 9% increase in quarterly revenue, despite a downturn in traffic in China that started earlier in the year.

Hermes is affected by the crisis in China, but the group is quite resilient, Chief Executive Axel Dumas said during an earnings call. "There are positive signs, but not enough to expect a full recovery," he added.

Hermes was one of the few companies that bucked a sector-wide slowdown in demand for luxury goods thanks to its exposure to the wealthiest shoppers, who have continued to splurge money on pricey accessories and clothes despite the economic environment.

Analysts point out that demand for the company's products is higher than supply, which enables Hermes to raise prices if needed. In light of the potential implementation of U.S. tariffs on European goods, the French group will be one of the handful of companies to have more room to maneuver and pass on import duties onto consumers through price hikes if needed, analysts said.

"One of the things that worries me the most is the evolution of geopolitical relations," Dumas said.

Customs duties have gone up and down and the company has increased prices to make up for the impact of those, the CEO said. The group's strategy is to hike prices to offset higher costs of production, monetary fluctuation and duties, he added.

Other brands could face more pressure, particularly those that cater to a less-wealthy clientele--the so-called aspirational shoppers--who pulled back on luxury purchases after years of price increases.

For the year as a whole, the group made earnings before interest and taxes of 6.15 billion euros compared with 5.65 billion euros in 2023. Net profit jumped to 4.60 billion euros from 4.31 billion euros, surpassing analysts' prospects of 4.51 billion euros.

The company confirmed its expectations for the medium term of revenue growth at constant exchange rates, but cautioned on global economic, geopolitical and monetary uncertainties.

Hermes proposed a dividend of 16 euros per share.

Write to Andrea Figueras at andrea.figueras@wsj.com

data.title: Coca-Cola Europacific Partners 2024 Earnings, Revenue Rise
data.description:
Coca-Cola Europacific Partners reported 2024 earnings Friday of 3.95 euros ($4.14) per diluted share, up from 3.71 euros a year earlier.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected 3.93 euros.

Revenue for the year ended Dec. 31 was 20.44 billion euros, up from 18.30 billion euros.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected 20.62 billion euros.

For 2025, the beverage company expects revenue growth of around 4%. Analysts polled by FactSet expect 21.60 billion euros.

data.title: Sompo International posts 93.7% CR as 9M underwriting profit dips
data.description:
(The Insurer) - Sompo International reported on Friday that its commercial P&C combined ratio deteriorated by 1.3 percentage points year on year to 93.7 percent during the first nine months of its fiscal year, while its gross written premiums climbed 4.5 percent.

  • SI Commercial P&C combined ratio of 93.7% (9M 2023: 92.4%)

  • SI NA combined ratio improves 7.9% year on year

  • SI Commercial P&C GWP increases 4.5%

The division’s commercial P&C operations – built out through its Japanese parent’s 2016 acquisition of Bermudian (re)insurer Endurance – reported GWP of $12.46bn for the first nine months of 2024.

This was an increase of $542mn year on year, largely driven by growth in the global markets and reinsurance segments and partially offset by lower commodity prices in AgriSompo.

Global markets reported the largest GWP increase with growth of 20.7 percent to $2.21bn. The carrier said the increase was driven by strong top-line growth across Europe, Middle East and Africa, Brazil, Asia Pacific and Turkey.

Reinsurance GWP jumped 9.5 percent to $4.2bn with the growth attributable to the successful renewal period and strong new business written.

By geography, North America posted the largest GWP increase with growth of 3.7 percent to $3.7bn, which Sompo said was a result of the rate and market environment and new business offset by additional reinsurance purchases.

In contrast, GWP in the AgriSompo business contracted by 13 percent to $2.2bn. Sompo said the unit had been negatively impacted by the “significant decrease” in commodity prices during the reporting period.

Sompo International’s nine-month P&C combined ratio of 93.7 percent included a 7.9 point improvement in its North America combined ratio but was offset by an 8.1 point decrease in AgriSompo.

The full-year combined ratio is expected to be 94.5 percent, the same as forecast at the mid-year point.

The combined ratio also included the impact of higher natural disaster losses, up to $262mn from the prior-year period’s $243mn.

Sompo International’s underwriting income fell to $438.8mn from $518.1mn in the prior-year period.

Adjusted profit within Sompo’s overseas insurance and reinsurance business increased by $198mn year on year to $1.06bn thanks to strong growth in underwriting income and net investment income.

For parent company Sompo Holdings, adjusted consolidated profit was ¥235.6bn ($1.5bn), down modestly from the ¥242.6bn posted in the prior-year period. This was a result of growth of underwriting income and net investment income in its overseas (re)insurance business.

data.title: Emaar Properties to Halt Trading in Dubai
data.description:
Emaar Properties (DFM:EMAAR) will pause trading on the Dubai Financial Market from 9:30 am local time on Friday due to a board meeting.

The Dubai-based property developer will resume trading once the meeting results are disclosed, according to a same-day release.

data.title: Kyrgyzstan Industrial Production Rises in January
data.description:
Industrial production in Kyrgyzstan climbed 10.7% year-on-year in January 2025, compared to a 16.5% rise in the same month of the previous year.

Output rose for manufacturing (9.6%), mining (2.5%), supply of electricity, gas and air conditioner (22.3%), and water supply and waste management (11.5%).

data.title: Acroud has successfully completed the previously announced restructurin
data.description:
Acroud AB

ACROUD AB (publ) (“Acroud” or the “Company”) has successfully completed the restructuring that was announced on 9 December 2024.

All shares issued pursuant to Acroud's resolution on a new issue of shares resolved on at the extraordinary general meeting held on 24 January 2025, have been subscribed for, allotted to, and payed for in accordance with the notice convening the extraordinary general meeting. Upon registration of the new issue of shares with the Swedish Companies Registration Office, the following entities will thus hold the following ownership stakes in Acroud: RIAE Media Ltd, approximately 39 per cent; Strategic Investments A/S, approximately 9.5 per cent; SMD Group Ltd, approximately 9.3 per cent; PMG Group A/S, approximately 6.2 per cent; Nordic Sports Management ApS, approximately 2.1 per cent; and Double Down Media Ltd, approximately 0.8 per cent. In addition, holders of the Company's bonds (the "Bondholders") with ISIN SE0017562481 (the "Bonds") will hold an ownership stake of approximately 23.4 per cent. The shares will be allotted to the Bondholders pro rata on the basis of total holdings on the record date on 7 February 2025. The total nominal amount of the Bonds is SEK 80,812,386 following the set-off against shares in an aggregate amount of SEK 70,000,114.

The Company's super-senior bonds

Acroud has on 29 January 2025 issued super-senior bonds with ISIN SE0023615661. The total nominal amount of the super-senior bonds is SEK 65,312,500, which has been paid by a cash payment of SEK 26,125,000 and in an amount of SEK 39,187,500 by way of set-off against Bonds.

Delivery and the admittance for trading of the new shares

All new issued shares are expected to be registered with the Swedish Companies Registration Office on 17 February 2025. The shares are expected to be delivered and admitted to trading on Nasdaq First North Growth Market on 21 February 2025.

Changes in share capital and number of shares

Resolution was made at the Company's extraordinary general meeting on 24 January 2025 to reduce the quota value to EUR 0.0038631 per share in order to enable the subscription price of SEK 0.25 per share in the Company's new issue. As a result of this change and the new issue of shares, the share capital of Acroud will increase from EUR 4,366,824.859242 to EUR 4,624,171.801175. The total number of shares in Acroud will increase from 172,612,188 to 1,196,998,584.

Amended terms and conditions for the Bonds

The amended and restated terms and conditions for the Bonds have been entered into force and are available on Acroud's website, https://www.acroud.com/en/corporate-governance/bond-issue-2022/.

Advisors

Pareto Securities AB is acting as financial advisor to Acroud and Gernandt & Danielsson Advokatbyrå is acting as legal advisor to Acroud.

For further information, please contact:

Robert Andersson, President and CEO

+356 9999 8017

Andrzej Mieszkowicz, CFO

+356 9911 2090

ACROUD AB (publ)

Telephone: +356 2132 3750/1

E-mail: info@acroud.com

Website: www.acroud.com

Certified Adviser: FNCA Sweden AB, info@fnca.se

 

From May 2024 (Q1 Report) Acroud has changed reporting and company language to English. This means that Interim Reports and the correlated press releases will be issued in English only.

About ACROUD AB

ACROUD is a fast-growing global challenger that operates and develops comparison and news sites within Poker, Sports Betting and Casino. Acroud also offers SaaS solutions for the iGaming affiliate industry. In past years, a number of companies have joined the ride and thus several experienced individuals in the industry leads Acroud's journey to become "The Mediahouse of The Future". Our mission is to connect people, Content Creators (Youtubers, Streamers, Affiliates) and businesses. We are growing fast and remain a leading global player in the industry with just over 70 people in Malta, United Kingdom, Denmark and Sweden. Acroud has been listed on the Nasdaq First North Growth Market since June 2018.

EN Acroud - Press release on completed recapitalisation on 14 February 2025 (ENG)(6718620.3)

data.title: Aircraft maintenance boom has further to fly
data.description:
In 2024, stuttering production of new jets saw repairs of older models take on fresh import for the likes of Melrose and Safran. This year deliveries could rise by 25%. But supply chain uncertainty and strong flying demand mean the maintenance profit engine can continue to motor.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow @Breakingviews on X

CONTEXT NEWS

France’s Safran reported full-year results on February 14. Adjusted revenue grew 17.8% year-on-year to 27.3 billion euros, while free cash flow rose from 2.9 billion euros to 3.2 billion euros.

Revenue in the company’s largest unit, Propulsion, whose activities include building and maintaining engines for civil and military aircraft, rose 15% on an organic basis to reach 13.7 billion euros, helped by civil aftermarket strength.

CEO Olivier Andriès said: “The operating margin grew by 150 basis points to 15.1% of sales, driven especially by strong aftermarket activity across the board, a relentless focus on operational excellence and the return to profitability of Aircraft Interiors”.

For 2025, the company raised previous outlooks for free cash flow and recurring operating income, helped by an increased forecast for growth in spare parts revenue.

GE Aerospace, Safran’s joint venture partner in engine maker CFM International, reported full-year results on January 23. Adjusted revenue rose 10% year-on-year to $35.1 billion. Its Commercial Engines and Services division grew 13%. The company forecast mid-teens growth in its CES unit in 2025.

Safran shares were up 0.5% by 0825 GMT on February 14.

data.title: German 10-Year Bund Yield Edges Up
data.description:
Germany’s 10-year Bund yield rose to 2.43% after US President Donald Trump signed a memorandum on reciprocal tariffs, though implementation was delayed to encourage negotiations.

Market sentiment also improved on hopes for a Russia-Ukraine peace deal, with Trump engaging Putin and Zelenskiy.

Meanwhile, money markets adjusted ECB rate expectations, pricing a December deposit rate of 1.94%, as investors await flash PMI to provide the first insights into how business conditions have fared in response to recent tariff announcements.

In the US, traders increased Fed rate cut bets to 33 basis points after inflation data.

In Germany, political uncertainty may persist after the February 23 federal election, as opposition leader Friedrich Merz and his CDU/CSU bloc will likely need to form a coalition to secure a majority.

data.title: Hoshizaki Logs 13% Boost in 2024 Profit
data.description:
Hoshizaki recorded a 13% rise in attributable profit for the year ended Dec. 31, 2024, to 37.2 billion yen from 32.8 billion yen in the year-ago period, a Thursday filing posted on the Tokyo bourse said.

Basic EPS was 258.49 yen in the year, up from 226.66 yen in the last fiscal year.

The industrial machinery company's net sales rose by 19% to 445.5 billion yen in the period from 373.6 billion yen a year prior.

The company declared a year-end dividend of 60 yen per share, bringing its total dividend for the fiscal year to 105 yen per share.

The company expects an attributable profit of 38.3 billion yen, a basic EPS of 270.84 yen, net sales of 460 billion yen, and a total dividend of 105 yen per share for the fiscal year ending Dec. 31.
